Biological Engineering · Class of 2026

Engineering biology into things that work — from the bench to the body.

I'm Gavin Murray, a Biological Engineering graduate who builds at the intersection of wet-lab research and computational modeling. Four years of coursework, research, and internships — organized here the way I'd want a reviewer to read it: problem, method, result.

View my work Download résumé
4years of coursework
3research & industry placements
6major projects shipped
"I wanted a field where solving a problem meant something for someone."

Why Biological Engineering? I came to this major wanting to combine the rigor of engineering with the complexity of living systems. What kept me here was the realization that most of the field's hardest problems — drug delivery, diagnostics, sustainable bioprocessing — need people who can move fluently between the bench and the model, between an assay and an algorithm.

This site walks through that path: the coursework that built my foundation, the research and internships that tested it, and the projects where I put it all together. My aim now is to bring that same problem-first approach to a team building real products, not just publishing results.

Capstone Engineer — 3D Printed Tactile Map of a Playground Using Photogrammetry

LSU Biological Engineering Senior Design (BE 4392), Advisor: Marybeth Lima

  • Built and validated the acquisition-to-fabrication pipeline (3DF Zephyr reconstruction, automated geometric refinement/feature extraction, tactile texture encoding, 3D-printed output) against a hand-modeled baseline
  • Achieved an 80.5% average time reduction (vs. a 75% goal), 90–94% dimensional accuracy, and an "excellent" System Usability Scale score of 87.5, tested with two prototype playgrounds and IRB-approved student feedback sessions at LSVI

03 — Case studies

Projects

Click a project to expand the problem, approach, and result. Replace details with your own work.

BE 4382 — Applications of Data Analysis and Modeling
Problem

Field goal attempts decide close NFL games, but public data only reports makes and attempts grouped into distance bins — not the exact distance of every kick — making it hard to model how success probability really changes with distance.

Approach

Built a joint Bayesian model in MATLAB: a Beta distribution to represent the unobserved distribution of attempt distances, and a logistic function to represent the probability of a make given distance. I derived the bin-level likelihood by integrating the Beta density and the logistic curve over each distance bin, wrote the negative log-posterior by hand, and used fminunc to compute the MAP estimate for all four parameters (α, β, γ₀, γ₁) with an uninformative prior. I then used fitglm to fit a weighted logistic regression against the Beta-derived expected yardage per bin as a check, and used trapz/integral for all the numerical integration.

Result

The fitted model put the average field goal attempt at 40.8 yards (mode 39.6, SD 11.7) and showed success probability falling from 99.4% at the goal line to 59.9% at 60 yards — with a 5-yard penalty at 45 yards costing kickers 6.3 percentage points of accuracy.

Individual project for Dr. Rego's data analysis and modeling course.

Final Report (PDF) ↗ Final Presentation (PDF) ↗
BE 3320 — Machine Design
Problem

Dental handpieces need to cut precisely at up to 30,000 rpm while staying light enough to avoid hand fatigue during long procedures — and safe enough to never fail mid-use.

Approach

Designed a bevel-gear drivetrain (32-tooth, 1:1 ratio, 20° pressure angle) in Fusion 360 to turn the motor's axial input 90° into the cutting bur. Verified every component by hand: AGMA bending and contact stress on the gears, then shaft stress via superposition, Von Mises, and the DE-Goodman fatigue criterion.

Result

Factors of safety of 7.8 (gear bending) and 3.4 (gear wear), with shaft safety factors of 8.6–200+ — all at a total device weight of 0.45 lb.

Design specifications
ParameterMax allowedValue chosen
Total handpiece length (Lₜ)130 mm122 mm
Head diameter (dₕ)22 mm22 mm
Head height (hₗ)26 mm26 mm
Handpiece body diameter (d)28 mm20 mm
Motor input shaft diameter (dₘ)7 mm7 mm
Bur shaft diameter (D_b)1.6 mm (fixed)1.6 mm
Required factor of safety≥ 23.4 – 200+
Skills demonstrated
Fusion 360AGMA Gear DesignShaft Fatigue (DE-Goodman)Material SelectionExcel Engineering Models

Team project with Brian Baudoin and Milain Marcel — I led the shaft stress analysis and drivetrain CAD.

Final Report (PDF) ↗ Presentation (PDF) ↗ Engineering Calculations (XLSX) ↗
BE 4392 — Senior Design (Biological Engineering Capstone)
Problem

The first tactile playground map built for the Louisiana School for the Visually Impaired (LSVI) in 2021 relied on manual measurements and hand-built CAD, making it far too slow and labor-intensive to replicate for other playgrounds.

Approach

Built a repeatable protocol that replaces manual measuring and modeling with drone/phone-based photogrammetry: reconstructing the playground in 3DF Zephyr, then automating geometric refinement, feature extraction (vertex classification, boundary reconstruction), and tactile texture encoding through a custom web app, before 3D printing in segmented, adhesive-jointed pieces.

Result

80.5% average time reduction versus the manual workflow (vs. a 75% goal), 90–94% dimensional accuracy against the hand-modeled baseline, and an "excellent" System Usability Scale score of 87.5 — validated on two prototype playgrounds and installed for testing at LSVI.

Meeting the design targets
Measurable objectiveTargetAchieved
Time reduction vs. manual process≥ 75%82% & 79% (2 playgrounds)
System Usability Scale score≥ 8087.5 — "excellent"
Dimensional accuracy vs. playground≥ 95% (85.4% revised threshold)90–94%
Single-piece 3D printYesNo — printed in 4 sections
Skills demonstrated
Photogrammetry (3DF Zephyr)Point Cloud ProcessingBlenderWeb App DevelopmentDFM / 3D PrintingUsability Testing (SUS)Economic AnalysisIRB Protocol Design

Six-person capstone team with Brian Baudoin, Milain Marcel, Katherine Mull, Anna Claire Ricks-Boyd, and Raedan Stephens — I led the time study, budget, and economic analysis, served as ordering manager, and was responsible for the dimensional accuracy assessments.
